subjectLine: The free AI that won't satisfice on your midterms
weekTopic: NotebookLM: Your Personal AI Study Partner

bigStory.headline: Google's NotebookLM Does What ChatGPT Can't
bigStory.summary: Here's the problem with ChatGPT: it mixes your sources with everything it learned from the internet. Ask about your class readings and you might get random Wikipedia facts instead. NotebookLM fixes this completely. It ONLY answers from materials you upload. No hallucinations. Real citations that trace back to your actual sources. You can upload 50 sources per notebook - lecture slides, PDFs, YouTube transcripts, Google Docs. And it's completely free.

classConnection: Midterms are coming. Imagine uploading every lecture slide, reading, and syllabus for a class, then asking NotebookLM to explain the trickiest concepts in plain English. Or having it generate a 2,000-word custom study guide structured exactly how you learn best. That's what the new Custom Reports feature does. Every answer comes with a citation you can click to verify. Your professors can't complain about AI-generated content when you're literally just studying your assigned readings more effectively.

threeThings.headline: Audio Overviews Turn Your Notes Into Podcasts
threeThings.summary: Upload your materials and NotebookLM generates a podcast-style summary with two AI hosts discussing your content. They debate, explain, and break down complex topics. Perfect for reviewing while commuting or working out. The new Interactive Mode even lets you jump in and ask questions mid-conversation - like having office hours on demand.
threeThings.takeaway: Create audio summaries of your class materials before exams. Listen during your commute instead of cramming at midnight.

threeThings.headline: Custom Reports Generate Professional Documents
threeThings.summary: This is the feature most people don't know about. NotebookLM can now generate structured reports - 2,000 to 3,000 words - with custom formatting, tone, and structure. Want a study guide that looks like a McKinsey deck? A competitive analysis formatted as an investor memo? You define the output format and it delivers.
threeThings.takeaway: Stop getting generic AI responses. Use Custom Reports to get deliverables you can actually submit or present.

threeThings.headline: Source Toggling Lets You Focus on What Matters
threeThings.summary: Have 30 sources but only need to study Chapters 5-8 for your exam? Toggle off the other sources. NotebookLM will only answer from what you've selected. This means you can separate midterm vs. final material, or compare what different authors say about the same topic.
threeThings.takeaway: Create one notebook per class with ALL materials, then toggle sources on/off based on which unit you're studying.

founderFramework.title: Build your AI study system (power-user edition)
founderFramework.intro: Most students use NotebookLM like ChatGPT - they ask basic questions and get basic answers. Here's how to actually leverage it. These prompts use features 90% of users don't know exist.

steps.title: Create a specification note that trains the AI
steps.description: Before asking any questions, create a 'spec note' that tells NotebookLM how you learn and what you need. This shapes every future response in that notebook.
steps.prompt:
Create a specification note for this notebook:

- Course: [COURSE NAME with professor]
- Exam format: [multiple choice / essay / case study / etc.]
- Professor's grading emphasis: [paste key phrases from syllabus]
- Concepts I struggle with: [list 2-3 topics]
- How I learn best: [examples / analogies / step-by-step / visual]
- Output format I prefer: [bullets / narrative / Q&A]

Use this context for all future responses. Prioritize what the professor emphasizes.

steps.title: Generate a custom study guide report
steps.description: Use the Custom Reports feature to create a comprehensive study guide. This isn't a basic summary - it's a structured document you could actually hand to someone else.
steps.prompt:
Generate a CUSTOM REPORT: Comprehensive Study Guide

Structure:
1. Executive Summary - The 5 things I absolutely must know (half page)
2. Concept Breakdown - Each major topic with:
   - Core definition in plain English
   - Real-world example I can reference in essays
   - How it connects to other concepts
3. Likely Exam Questions - Based on what the professor emphasized
4. Model Answers - How a top student would answer each
5. Common Mistakes - Where students typically lose points
6. Quick Reference - One-page cheat sheet format

Tone: Like a senior who got an A explaining to a freshman
Length: Detailed (2,000+ words)

steps.title: Run an exam simulation with grading
steps.description: Don't just review - test yourself. This prompt creates a realistic exam, grades your answers, and teaches you what you missed.
steps.prompt:
Act as my professor for [COURSE NAME]. Create a realistic practice exam:

Part 1: Concept Check (5 questions)
- Multiple choice with 4 options each
- Include one 'trick' question the professor would use
- After I answer, explain why wrong answers are wrong

Part 2: Application (3 questions)
- Short answer requiring specific examples from readings
- Grade on a 10-point scale with specific feedback

Part 3: Synthesis (1 question)
- Essay question connecting multiple concepts
- Provide a grading rubric before I answer
- After I answer, grade me and show what an A+ answer includes

Start with Part 1. Wait for my answers before moving on.

founderFramework.bottomLine: These prompts took me from using NotebookLM as a fancy search bar to actually understanding my material. The exam simulation alone is worth the 15 minutes of setup.
founderFramework.personalNote:
Real talk - my actual setup:

One notebook per class. Everything labeled by date. Before each class I create a quick summary of the readings - not to skip them, this supplements the actual work. I review it right before class and right after.

Before exams: I select only the sources being tested and generate multiple study guides, not just one. NotebookLM misses things, so I run 2-3 different prompts to catch everything. Then I create an Audio Overview and listen walking to the exam.

aiInBusiness.title: NotebookLM for Your Startup

workflows.title: Competitive intelligence brief
workflows.description: Upload competitor websites, pitch decks, reviews, and industry reports. Get a structured analysis that looks like it came from a consulting firm.
workflows.prompt:
Generate a CUSTOM REPORT: Competitive Analysis Brief

Context: I'm building [YOUR STARTUP - one sentence]

Structure:
1. Market Landscape - Who are the players and what's total market size
2. Feature Matrix - Table comparing what each competitor offers
3. Pricing Analysis - How each charges and their positioning
4. Gap Analysis - What NO ONE is doing well (my opportunity)
5. Differentiation Strategy - How I should position against each
6. Risks - What could go wrong if I enter this market

Tone: McKinsey consultant briefing a CEO
Format: Executive summary up front, details after
Length: Detailed with comparison tables

workflows.title: Investor meeting one-pager
workflows.description: Upload the VC's website, portfolio page, partner bios, and any interviews. Get personalized talking points for your specific meeting.
workflows.prompt:
Generate a CUSTOM REPORT: Investor Intelligence Brief

Meeting: [INVESTOR NAME] at [FIRM NAME]

Structure:
1. Firm Profile - Investment thesis, typical check size, stage focus
2. This Partner's Background - Their history and what they care about
3. Recent Investments - What they've funded in last 12 months
4. Portfolio Synergies - Their companies I could partner with or reference
5. Likely Questions - What they'll probably ask based on their thesis
6. My Talking Points - How to connect my startup to their pattern
7. Things to Avoid - Red flags based on their public statements

Tone: Internal strategy memo before a big meeting
Length: 1,500 words, scannable with clear headers

workflows.title: Customer research synthesis
workflows.description: Upload interview transcripts, survey responses, support tickets, or reviews. Get patterns and exact quotes you can use in your pitch.
workflows.prompt:
Generate a CUSTOM REPORT: Voice of Customer Analysis

Structure:
1. Top Pain Points - Ranked by how often they appear (with frequency)
2. Exact Quotes - The specific words customers use (for my landing page)
3. Jobs to Be Done - What are they actually trying to accomplish
4. Current Solutions - What they use now and why it fails them
5. Willingness to Pay - Any signals about budget or price sensitivity
6. Persona Clusters - Group similar customers together
7. Messaging Recommendations - What to say based on their language

Tone: Product manager presenting findings to founders
Format: Highlight direct quotes in each section
Length: Detailed, with quotes pulled directly from sources

aiInBusiness.personalNote:
How I use this for Varity:

All my market research, customer surveys, and competitive intel lives in NotebookLM - labeled like folders so I can toggle between topics. I keep roadmap files in there and update them as I make progress.

When I need a deck, brief, or any document, I use Deep Research mode. Everything it generates is specific to MY startup because it's pulling from MY data. No generic advice.

quickHits: OpenAI raised $110B at a $730B valuation on Thursday. Amazon put in $50B, Nvidia $30B, SoftBank $30B. It's the largest private funding round in history.
Anthropic refused to let the Pentagon use Claude for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons. Trump blacklisted them Friday. Hours later, OpenAI signed a Pentagon deal with similar guardrails.
Block (Square, Cash App) is laying off 40% of its workforce - over 4,000 people - citing AI. Jack Dorsey said most companies will reach the same conclusion. The white-collar recession is here.
Nvidia's CEO said $3-4 trillion will be spent on AI infrastructure by 2030. On their earnings call, he called it 'the agentic AI inflection point.' Your next job might be in AI ops.
nextWeek: The AI layoff wave just got real - Block cut 40% of staff citing AI, and Dorsey says most companies will follow. I'll break down which jobs are actually safe, which skills matter now, and how to make yourself irreplaceable.
